translation.content: When you think of London label and party Cartulis, dark, muscular electro and tough tech house springs to mind. But the name is more revealing than it first appears: in the founder's formerly-adopted home of Madrid, cartulis translates to "nerd." Like any labour of love, the project is driven by a deep, obsessive dedication, centred around sound, mood and community. Recorded at Cartulis' 15th anniversary party in 2024, RA.1017 captures why Trotti is such a compelling DJ. Playing only vinyl, he has a deft ability to balance functional club pressure with more leftfield selections, and as he outlines in his Q&A below, it comes from a belief in creative limitation, committing to a finite set of records and trusting instinct over abundance. Across the two hours, there are plenty of big, physical tracks built for the dance floor, but also some genuinely strange, confrontational stretches. Around the 15-minute mark, church bells ring out as a gothic female vocal looms overhead—David Lynch, if he needed an electro soundtrack. It's the kind of material you only hear at parties where dancers feel truly at ease, unafraid of the weird. That's Cartulis. The fact Cartulis has fostered this kind of trust over 15 years is no small achievement in a city like London, where competition makes building a real community hard. But Trotti's principles have remained steadfast since he first arrived from Madrid in 2008, initially working as a sound technician before founding the party a year later. For him, "connection matters more than profit" and the overall aim is to create "a space rooted in care, connection and shared experience." That's how you build a legacy that lasts. Here's to another 15 years. - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I've been incredibly busy, always juggling a lot at the same time. My main job is DJing and everything that comes with it: digging, travelling, preparing sets, staying inspired. I'm also working on Cartulis, both the event and the label, where I am the creative director. We're planning next year's releases and shaping the upcoming season of events. There's a lot in motion: new music from artists like Innuendo, DMX Krew, plus a quadruple-pack vinyl compilation we've been curating for quite a while. On the events side, we're running five shows a year in London, along with showcases in other cities and festivals, something I'm building together with Sabri, my best friend and partner. Another big focus has been developing my new live set for Hackney Electronica. We're releasing our second record on Dark Entries and debuting the show at Cartulis in London. And, on top of everything, we've just moved our bakery, Weirdough, to a new location in Walthamstow—another massive project that's been taking a lot of energy. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? This mix is very special to me. It was recorded live at the Cartulis 15th Anniversary in London on Saturday November 23rd, 2024. It was the closing set of the night, and it captured the energy, emotions and magic of that moment, a memory of what that celebration felt like. I'm a vinyl DJ, so for this occasion I spent hours preparing a record bag with some of the most meaningful records I play at the moment and that felt fitting for the occasion. What I love about this format is how it limits me—in a good way. It forces me to commit to a direction instead of getting lost in endless options and it pushes me to make conscious decisions of what I want to transmit all along. What are you looking forward to in the near future? For me, the future is about care and connection. I hope we, as a community, can look out for each other, beyond ego and individualism. Our scene, like the world, is often driven by competition, power and short-term thinking. I'd love to see big clubs, promoters and festivals working together to nurture the culture we love, to teach the next generation what rave and music truly mean, instead of focusing on exclusivity through contracts, barriers for smaller promoters or self-interest. I hope people are booked and recognised for their artistry and energy, not just their names. Especially nowadays, when so many bookings are influenced more by social media presence or other superficial factors than by artistic skill or values. On a personal level, I just want to continue doing what I love and keep growing, both as a human and as an artist. I want to stay close to my friends, keep discovering and experimenting with sound, and let music continue to guide me. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? The need to recentre community as a political value. We're living in a world shaped by hyperindividualism and economic systems that reward competition over care, and it's creating isolation, burnout and a deep sense of disconnection for all of us. I believe a more equal and humane society begins with rebuilding the bonds between people, strengthening empathy, solidarity and mutual support. These aren't just emotional aspirations; they're political acts that push back against the structures that keep us divided. Music and culture already show us what this can look like: spaces where people come together across differences, where connection matters more than profit. Those moments remind us of the world we could build. I think our responsibility is to cultivate that energy, extend it beyond our scene, and make community itself a form of resistance and equality. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? The parties that shaped me the most are the ones where music sits at the centre of everything. Spaces like Houghton Festival, Phonoteque, Bonanza Festival, Cobalt Studios, Ring, and many more… all showed me different ways people can connect through sound. I also have to mention FOLD, for me, one of the best clubs in the world. I love the sound, the layout, the lights… everything feels aligned with the music. But above all, it's Cartulis. Cartulis is home. It's where I learned how to DJ, where I watched my heroes play, and where I understood how music can build a space rooted in care, connection and shared experience. It's the place that taught me what a real community on a dance floor feels like.
translation.blurb: The London-via-Bilbao DJ marks 15 years of Cartulis with two hours of punchy house, recorded live at FOLD.
